metrics.narrative: David Hogsden quantified the operational pain in detail without yet stating a $/£ ROI figure: two full-time staff manually build 30-40 payment files a week across 140 bank accounts ('it is not scalable what we have in that space, and my team spent more time preparing payments than we do processing invoices', Avoma call 3 Jun 2026), and roughly 700 invoices sit outstanding with the business at any time due to missing POs, insufficient funds, or disputes (Avoma call 17 Jun 2026). Yondr is also planning to 4-5x its legal-entity count over 3-4 years, which will multiply this pain if unaddressed (call 27 May 2026). Quantifying this into a dollarized business case is the open move.

champion.narrative: David Hogsden is the active champion: he set the agenda on every call, coordinated demo scheduling and sample data, and per the rep's early-Jul Next Step 'has a good relationship with the relevant stakeholder has developed: consistent internal advocacy and detailed requirements gathering across five touchpoints. What is still missing: David is an interim/contractor (email domain shows '-ext'), has not secured Zycus a route to Sandy, and by his own account the rest of the team 'really like Zycus' but 'have not had the deep dive with Ren yet' — meaning his advocacy has not yet been tested against the competing POC.

competition.narrative: Yondr ran a 5-vendor field (Zycus, Airwallix, Payhawk, Hughes, Yooz/Quadian referenced) that dropped Hughes, Yooz and Quadian by 3 Jun 2026 for lacking AI focus, narrowing to Zycus, Airwallix, Payhawk and Zip by 24 Jun 2026. The wedge is clear: Zycus wins on purchasing UX and AI depth but has no native payments module, which the buyer called 'a real downer' (3 Jun 2026); Zip is now the closest procurement-overlap threat with near-identical pricing per the rep's early-Jul Next Step; and Renpayments is the newest and most urgent threat because the CFO is personally running a POC with them outside Zycus's visibility.

identify_pain.narrative: Confirmed, specific, and owned pain: David Hogsden's AP team touches every invoice 3 to 8 times before payment and manually builds 30-40 payment files weekly across 140 bank accounts, a process he calls 'not scalable' as Yondr's PE owner plans to 4-5x the business (calls 27 May and 3 Jun 2026). Robert Greig separately owns the pain of standing up new legal entities, calling it 'very, very painful' due to repeated manual configuration (27 May 2026). Casual end users struggle with raw D365 PO creation, relying on guides and training (27 May 2026).

paper_process.narrative: No MSA, legal, procurement-channel, or InfoSec conversation has occurred yet across any of the five calls, and Next_Step__c only shows 'currently creating commercials and PS ' as of early Jul 2026 — this is normal for a deal still in Formal Evaluation/vendor-selection and not yet a red flag. Visibility will come once the commercial pack under active preparation is delivered and the buyer moves into its stated Jul-Aug 2026 contracting window.

economic_buyer.narrative: The CFO, named 'Sandy', is confirmed as the final decision maker who will review 2-3 shortlisted vendor demos (Avoma call 3 Jun 2026: 'CFO is primary decision maker, reviewing 2-3 deep-dive demos by mid-to-late June'). However Sandy has never joined a Zycus call across all five touchpoints, and per the rep's most recent Next Step (early Jul 2026) is now personally running a 2-week proof-of-concept with Renpayments, a level of direct engagement Zycus has not yet earned. Securing the offered 'lunch meeting' with Sandy (referenced 24 Jun 2026 and again in the early-Jul Next Step) is the critical open move.

decision_process.narrative: The call sequence itself maps the process: discovery (27 May) -> functional demo (29 May) -> mid-cycle check-in narrowing 5 vendors to 3-4 (3 Jun) -> deep-dive demo with a wider Yondr team including a compliance/regulated-entity voice (17 Jun) -> status call narrowing to 2 finalists for CFO review (24 Jun). David Hogsden and Robert Greig run the evaluation day to day; the VP of Finance (David's line manager) reviews the deep dives; Sandy makes the final call after seeing the top two, likely alongside other C-suite per call 1 ('it will see suite... just high level'). No formal RFP has been confirmed, only an informal high-level comparison against a ~£250k threshold.

decision_criteria.narrative: The buyer has been explicit: best-in-class end-user purchasing experience ('Out of all the solutions we've seen, the end user experience is probably the best', David, 3 Jun 2026), strong backend master-data handling for legal entities, native AI/agentic capability, and critically, payments/EFT capability. Price is now a hard gate given zero available budget ('our budget is zero... get it as cheap as you can for us', David, 24 Jun 2026). The buyer is deliberately pairing a procurement-strong finalist (Zycus or Zip) against a payments-strong finalist (Airwallix or Payhawk) to compare trade-offs.

engagement.top_event: Schedule deep-dive demo / workshop (60-90 mins, P2P focus: guided intake, PO approval, invoice automation, approval context, exception routing, D365 integration)

day_summary.overall: The pivotal update: David told Claire that Zycus's implementation quote is running 50% higher than Zip's, and that Yondr will decide next week which vendors advance to the final round. Claire moved immediately the same day, emailing David that Zycus can drop the implementation cost from its initial GBP140k to GBP100k fixed, paid against milestones, after clearing it with SLT. Platform pricing is already near-parity with Zip, so implementation cost was the one exposed gap and Zycus has now closed most of it going into the finalist decision. Separately, the buyer (29 Jun) asked Zycus to complete an AI Questionnaire as part of its review, which is still outstanding.

items.detail: Zycus has no native payment or EFT module, which is the buyer's #1 operational pain (eliminate 30-40 manual weekly payment runs, automate routing across 140 bank accounts). Buyer stated 'I thought you guys could do payments and I think that was a real downer' (3 June). Zycus payment platform is in development (wireframes exist, no launch date, no reference customers per Nishita 3 June). Mitigation offered: partner integration (e.g. Transfermate per 24 June) or build pre-contract, but buyer said 'budget is zero… get it as cheap as you can' so partner cost may be prohibitive. If Zycus cannot close payment gap at competitive price, buyer may select payment-focused finalist (Airwallix or Payhawk) over Zycus.

Zycus has no native expenses management module. Buyer wants 'one solution for all purchasing… PO supplies company cards reimbursable expenses payments' (27 May). Expenses currently in D365; buyer would prefer to consolidate but stated 'we can still implement another solution to do expenses… the main focus is purchasing' (3 Jun), so this is an acceptable gap, lower risk than payments gap. Not a blocker.

Buyer has zero budget post-ERP implementation and explicitly requested 'get it as cheap as you can' (24 June). Budget threshold is ~£250k for RFP per Next_Step__c. Zycus commercial pack not yet delivered (due 27 June). If Zycus price is not competitive vs Zip, Payhawk, Airwallix (all of whom have not submitted pricing yet either per 24 June), buyer may select on price alone given budget constraint. Risk: payment partner integration cost on top of Zycus license may push total cost above competitor all-in pricing.

Zip is the only other procurement-focused platform in final four and directly competes on Zycus's differentiated strength (purchasing UX, AI). Buyer stated Zip 'does look good and similar but slightly different' (24 June). If Zip's AI capability and roadmap are comparable to Zycus and price is lower, Zip may win the procurement-finalist slot. Mitigation: de-position Zip on AI depth and offer to build any missing features free pre-contract.

Renpayments (Ren) is running a CFO-sponsored 2-week POC as of mid-June (Next_Step__c dated ~15 June). Outcome unknown. If Ren POC is successful, CFO may favour Ren regardless of David/Robert's recommendation. 'Rest of the team really like Zycus, they have not had the deep dive with Ren yet' suggests internal support for Zycus, but CFO has final authority. Mitigation: move fast on commercial and feature commit before Ren POC concludes; if Ren is payment-only, contrast Zycus end-to-end P2P.

Buyer stated 'budget is zero, get it as cheap as you can' (24 Jun) and 'pricing wise we are very similar to Zip' (Next_Step__c early Jul), so cost is the tiebreaker. Deliver pack immediately showing Zycus + Transfermate total cost is competitive with Airwallix/Ren all-in pricing. Without it, buyer cannot finalize decision and may select competitor on incomplete data.

Zycus is in a live bake-off where the CFO is personally running a Renpayments POC without ever having taken a Zycus call; converting the offered CFO lunch meeting into a real touchpoint before the 2-week POC ends is the single highest-leverage move.

The payments gap remains the buyer's stated #1 concern ('that was a real downer', 3 Jun 2026); closing the loop on Transfermate's exact mechanics and cost is what converts the UX preference into a win over Airwallix and Payhawk.

The opportunity is single-threaded on an interim/contractor champion (David Hogsden, '-ext' email) with only one buyer-side contact role on the record; capturing Robert Greig and the VP of Finance as formal opportunity contacts protects against David's rotation risk.
best_practice_check.summary: Three levers matter most right now: closing the CFO access gap before the Renpayments POC concludes, finishing the payments/Transfermate story, and broadening the buying coalition beyond one interim contact.

competitive_position.summary: Zip is now the explicit head-to-head procurement rival: on 10 Jul David told Zycus its implementation was 50% higher than Zip and that Yondr will pick finalists next week. Zycus responded by cutting implementation from GBP140k to GBP100k fixed (paid against milestones) to close the gap; platform pricing is already near-parity with Zip. The decision axis is procurement (Zycus P2P) vs a narrower payments play. Renpayments (CFO POC) and payment-focused finalists (Airwallix, Payhawk) remain in the mix but Zip is the deciding comparison on cost. Renpayments is the single strongest CURRENT threat: as of the rep's early-Jul 2026 Next Step, the CFO (Sandy) is personally running a 2-week proof-of-concept with them, a level of direct EB engagement Zycus has not achieved. Zip is the closest procurement-overlap peer, with pricing now reported near-identical to Zycus's (early Jul 2026). Airwallix and Payhawk remain live payment-focused finalists as of 24 Jun 2026 but pose lower overlap risk since the buyer explicitly rates Zycus's purchasing UX above theirs. Hughes, Yooz and Quadian were eliminated by 3 Jun 2026 for lacking AI focus. The legacy stack (D365 plus xFlow OCR and SK Global payments) is the incumbent being displaced, with the buyer explicit about wanting to eliminate xFlow and SK Global (27 May 2026).

win_position.text: Zycus is at price parity on the platform, rated best purchasing UX of five vendors, and responded to the cost gap within the same day with a GBP100k fixed implementation counter — keeping it live for the finalist round.

deltas.subject: Zycus has no native payment or EFT module, which is the buyer's #1 operational pain (eliminate 30-40 manual weekly payment runs, automate routing across 140 bank accounts). Mitigation offered: partner integration (e.g. Transfermate per 24 June) or build pre-contract, but buyer said 'budget is zero… get it as cheap as you can' so partner cost may be prohibitive. Provide RFP primer outlining must-have P2P requirements

Zycus has no native expenses management module. Buyer wants 'one solution for all purchasing… PO supplies company cards reimbursable expenses payments' (27 May). Expenses currently in D365; buyer would prefer to consolidate but stated 'we can still implement another solution to do expenses… the main focus is purchasing' (3 June), so this is acceptable gap, lower risk than payments gap.

De-position Zip on AI maturity and feature roadmap before CFO demos (early July): Zip is the only other procurement-platform finalist and the direct peer threat on Zycus's differentiated strength (purchasing UX, AI). Buyer stated Zip 'does look good and similar but slightly different' (24 June). Action: in the commercial pack (due 27 June) or a follow-up call, contrast Zycus 714 agentic agents (live, customer-proven per Coca-Cola Porsche Cargoitalia references from discovery deck) against Zip's intake-orchestration positioning (per Claire's note Zip is 'bolt-on' not full platform). Offer to build any Zip feature Yondr values, free, pre-contract (Nishita commitment 24 June). Provide a side-by-side AI capability matrix (autonomous negotiation, touchless invoicing, conversational interface) with customer proof points. Timing: this must land before shortlist decision (week of 30 June) to ensure Zycus is the procurement finalist, not Zip.

Deliver the commercial pack by Friday 27 June at the lowest defensible price to secure first-mover advantage and finalist slot: Buyer stated 'our budget is zero… get it as cheap as you can' and 'we haven't actually received any prices from any of the potential solutions yet' (24 June). Price is explicitly a decision factor in a zero-budget environment post-ERP. Zycus has opportunity to anchor the buyer's price expectation by being first to submit a compelling commercial (software license for P2P suite ideally under the £250k RFP threshold noted in Next_Step__c, plus transparent implementation cost). Action: Claire to finalize and send commercial pack including payment partner integration options and total cost of ownership by end of Friday 27 June as committed on 24 June call. Emphasize value (AI, feature build, master-data integration, references) to justify price and offer flexible payment terms if needed to fit budget constraints.

Secure Anthony Gray (the deal owner's manager, Zycus VP Europe) for CFO (Sandy) demo if Zycus is shortlisted to final two, to build exec-level relationship and de-risk commercial negotiation: The CFO is the Economic Buyer and final decision-maker (stated 3 June and 24 June). David and Robert are the evaluators and will recommend two vendors, but CFO approval is the gate. Anthony Gray was on the discovery call 27 May and holds the regional exec relationship. Buyer has stated 'our CFO… we will decide who we put in front of him' (24 June). Action: if Zycus is shortlisted (known early July), offer an exec briefing or demo with Anthony Gray + Claire + Nishita for the CFO, positioned as strategic partnership discussion (scale, roadmap, commitment to build features, long-term P2P vision). This de-risks price negotiation (exec can offer flexibility), builds C-level confidence (peer-to-peer), and differentiates Zycus from vendor-only demos. Timing: offer this in the commercial pack or immediately upon shortlist confirmation.

Timing: this must land before shortlist decision (week of 30 June) to ensure Zycus is the procurement finalist, not Zip.
